## Findings

### F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-001 — Volatility3 windows.info on the nfury memory image reports NTBu… *(confidence: HIGH)*

Volatility3 windows.info on the nfury memory image reports NTBuildLab 7601.17727.amd64fre.win7sp1_gdr., Is64Bit True, KeNumberProcessors 1, NtSystemRoot C:\Windows, NtProductType NtProductWinNt, and SystemTime 2012-04-06 21:28:39+00:00.. The acquired memory image is a single-CPU 64-bit Windows 7 SP1 (build 7601.17727) system whose in-image clock read 2012-04-06 21:28:39 UTC. This establishes the OS/build baseline and the reference time for all subsequent memory artifacts on host nfury (10.3.58.6)..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-memory/win7-64-nfury-memory-raw.001#KUSER_SHARED_DATA / KdVersionBlock; SystemTime 2012-04-06 21:28:39+00:00` — tool `volatility3 windows.info`, command `vol -f /tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-memory/win7-64-nfury-memory-raw.001 windows.info`
- **Cited evidence:** `NTBuildLab	7601.17727.amd64fre.win7sp1_gdr.`, `Is64Bit	True`, `SystemTime	2012-04-06 21:28:39+00:00`, `NtSystemRoot	C:\Windows`, `NtProductType	NtProductWinNt` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 f3d3de8185564f70…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-001`.
- **Council Receipt:** `748a3efd9c81c7c3…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-002 — Volatility3 windows.netscan on the nfury memory image reports t… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

Volatility3 windows.netscan on the nfury memory image reports three TCP endpoints from this host to internal address 10.3.58.4: System (PID 4) 10.3.58.6:49325 -> 10.3.58.4:139 CLOSED; lsass.exe (PID 552) :49285 -> 10.3.58.4:135 CLOSED; and lsass.exe (PID 552) :49286 -> 10.3.58.4:49156 CLOSED.. Host nfury (10.3.58.6) initiated SMB/NetBIOS (139), RPC endpoint-mapper (135), and a dynamically-assigned RPC port (49156) connections to a single internal peer, 10.3.58.4. SMB plus RPC/EPM plus a high dynamic port directed from this host at one internal peer is consistent with host-to-host SMB/RPC access such as remote administration or lateral movement. The fact that lsass.exe (not a user process) owns the RPC sockets is notable. netscan residual sockets alone cannot establish intent, success, or which host was compromised first..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-memory/win7-64-nfury-memory-raw.001#netscan offsets 0x7dbad5e0 (System->139), 0x7dc7d650 (lsass->135), 0x7d8b8cf0 (lsass->49156)` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -f /tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-memory/win7-64-nfury-memory-raw.001 windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `10.3.58.6	49325	10.3.58.4	139	CLOSED	4	System`, `49285	10.3.58.4	135	CLOSED	552	lsass.exe`, `49286	10.3.58.4	49156	CLOSED	552	lsass.exe` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 01e869325f860643…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-002`.
- **Council Receipt:** `b99bc0e6863c29c4…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-003 — Volatility3 windows.netscan reports svchost.exe (PID 408) LISTE… *(confidence: HIGH)*

Volatility3 windows.netscan reports svchost.exe (PID 408) LISTENING on 0.0.0.0:3389 (TCPv4 and TCPv6), and f-response-ent (PID 328) with an ESTABLISHED TCP session 10.3.58.6:3260 -> 10.3.16.5:48769 plus LISTENING on 0.0.0.0:3260.. Remote Desktop (TCP 3389) was enabled and listening on nfury at capture time, providing a remote-access surface. The ESTABLISHED 3260 (iSCSI) session and 3260 listener belong to f-response-ent (PID 328, created 21:25:10, just before capture), i.e. the live remote forensic acquisition channel to the examiner host 10.3.16.5 - this is acquisition infrastructure, not attacker activity, and is recorded here to distinguish it from intrusion traffic..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-memory/win7-64-nfury-memory-raw.001#netscan offsets 0x7dbdd470 (3389 LISTEN, PID 408), 0x7f715cf0 (3260 ESTABLISHED, PID 328)` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -f /tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-memory/win7-64-nfury-memory-raw.001 windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `3389	0.0.0.0	0	LISTENING	408	svchost.exe`, `10.3.58.6	3260	10.3.16.5	48769	ESTABLISHED	328	f-response-ent` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 01e869325f860643…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-003`.
- **Council Receipt:** `aa84d701610278dc…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-004 — ASCII strings extracted from the $DATA of NTFS inode 80517 (ide… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

ASCII strings extracted from the $DATA of NTFS inode 80517 (identified by fls/istat as Users/vibranium/AppData/Local/Temp/a.exe, a 9216-byte PE) include the embedded PDB path c:\code\httppump\inner\objchk_wxp_x86\i386\i.pdb and the imported APIs WriteProcessMemory, GetCurrentProcess, GetProcAddress, LoadLibraryA, VirtualAlloc and CreateThread.. a.exe is a small (~9 KB) standalone PE staged in a user's Temp directory whose import set (VirtualAlloc + WriteProcessMemory + CreateThread + LoadLibraryA + GetProcAddress) is the canonical primitive sequence for allocating memory and injecting/executing shellcode in a process. It carries a non-vendor developer PDB path (c:\code\httppump\...\i.pdb) and no legitimate publisher metadata. This is consistent with a malicious code-injection loader/stager rather than legitimate software. Static strings alone do not prove it executed or identify its C2..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01#NTFS inode 80517; Users/vibranium/AppData/Local/Temp/a.exe` — tool `sleuthkit icat + strings`, command `icat /tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01 80517 | strings`
- **Cited evidence:** `c:\code\httppump\inner\objchk_wxp_x86\i386\i.pdb`, `WriteProcessMemory`, `VirtualAlloc`, `CreateThread`, `LoadLibraryA`, `GetProcAddress` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 1c622d69294c39af…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-004`.
- **Council Receipt:** `cd7e5b6035061151…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-005 — fls of NTFS inode 92258 (Users/vibranium/AppData/Local/Temp/_ME… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

fls of NTFS inode 92258 (Users/vibranium/AppData/Local/Temp/_MEI65402) lists python27.dll, pywintypes27.dll, win32api.pyd, _socket.pyd, _ssl.pyd, _hashlib.pyd, select.pyd, unicodedata.pyd, bz2.pyd, msvcr90.dll and a shell.exe.manifest.. _MEI65402 is the runtime self-extraction directory of a PyInstaller 'onefile' executable: the _MEI<digits> naming convention plus a bundled CPython 2.7 runtime (python27.dll) and its dependency .pyd/.dll modules are produced only when such a packaged executable is run. The bundle includes networking and TLS modules (_socket.pyd, _ssl.pyd, _hashlib.pyd) and Windows API access (win32api.pyd, pywintypes27.dll), and the embedded application manifest identifies the original program as 'shell' (shell.exe.manifest). This indicates a packaged Python remote-shell/agent was extracted and run under the vibranium profile. The directory does not by itself reveal the C2 address or the shell's exact capabilities..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01#NTFS inode 92258; Users/vibranium/AppData/Local/Temp/_MEI65402` — tool `sleuthkit fls`, command `fls -p /tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01 92258`
- **Cited evidence:** `python27.dll`, `pywintypes27.dll`, `win32api.pyd`, `_socket.pyd`, `_ssl.pyd`, `shell.exe.manifest` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 07663299fd86dbbb…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-005`.
- **Council Receipt:** `48493870fb824564…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-007 — ASCII strings from the recycled binary svchost.exe (NTFS inode … *(confidence: HIGH)*

ASCII strings from the recycled binary svchost.exe (NTFS inode 92255, istat Name 'svchost.exe') include the registry path System\CurrentControlSet\Services\netman\domain, the value names home and pause, a hardcoded URL http://192.168.1.5/ads/, the string 'The URI must begin with "https://" || "http://".', and the PDB path d:\work\hg\httppump\winclient\objfre_wxp_x86\i386\winclient.pdb.. This binary is the implant body. Its embedded references to System\CurrentControlSet\Services\netman\domain with home/pause exactly match the winclient.reg C2 configuration (F-...-006), showing the binary reads its C2 URL and beacon interval from that key; http://192.168.1.5/ads/ is the hardcoded default C2/URI. Its PDB path lies under a 'httppump\winclient' project, the same 'httppump' codebase as the PDB in a.exe (F-...-004, c:\code\httppump\inner\...\i.pdb) - linking the two artifacts to one HTTP-RAT toolset. The file is named svchost.exe but resides outside System32 (in a Recycle Bin), i.e. it masquerades as the legitimate Windows service host.. This is the **corrected** finding — an initial, stronger reading was refuted by the Council (see *Self-correction*, below) and the analyst re-filed only what the evidence supports.

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01#NTFS inode 92255; $Recycle.Bin/S-1-5-...-1673/$R6SODDB/svchost.exe` — tool `sleuthkit icat + strings`, command `icat /tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01 92255 | strings`
- **Cited evidence:** `System\CurrentControlSet\Services\netman\domain`, `http://192.168.1.5/ads/`, `d:\work\hg\httppump\winclient\objfre_wxp_x86\i386\winclient.pdb`, `home`, `pause` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 2a59a2143a42855c…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-007`.
- **Council Receipt:** `1763f46c12989232…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-008 — The Windows Recycle Bin $I metadata file $I6SODDB (NTFS inode 9… *(confidence: HIGH)*

The Windows Recycle Bin $I metadata file $I6SODDB (NTFS inode 91913) records the original path of the deleted item as C:\Windows\System32\dllhost.. The deleted folder $R6SODDB - which contains the implant svchost.exe (F-...-007) and its C2 config winclient.reg (F-...-006) - originally resided at C:\Windows\System32\dllhost. A folder named 'dllhost' placed directly in System32 masquerades as Windows component naming, and System32 is a privileged location requiring administrative rights to write. The whole malware folder was subsequently deleted into the Recycle Bin of the SID ending -1673. This identifies the implant's staging directory and shows an attempt to remove it (anti-forensics), with the contents nonetheless recoverable.. This is the **corrected** finding — an initial, stronger reading was refuted by the Council (see *Self-correction*, below) and the analyst re-filed only what the evidence supports.

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01#NTFS inode 91913; $Recycle.Bin/S-1-5-...-1673/$I6SODDB` — tool `sleuthkit icat + strings -el`, command `icat /tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01 91913 | strings -el`
- **Cited evidence:** `C:\Windows\System32\dllhost` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 a074a85b3b63ae7b…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-008`.
- **Council Receipt:** `66866b32a586fb06…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-009 — A recovered registry-import file (winclient.reg, NTFS inode 922… *(confidence: HIGH)*

A recovered registry-import file (winclient.reg, NTFS inode 92256, recycled) contains: [HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Netman\domain] with values "home"="http://199.73.28.114/ads/" and "pause"=dword:00000020.. This .reg file is structured to configure malware command-and-control by writing under the Netman service key (CurrentControlSet\Services\Netman\domain): 'home' holds the C2 callback URL http://199.73.28.114/ads/ and 'pause' (0x20 = 32) appears to hold a beacon/sleep interval. Storing such settings under a legitimate service's subkey (Netman) is consistent with a concealment/persistence technique. This is consistent with http://199.73.28.114/ads/ being the C2 endpoint specified for the implant. As a standalone .reg artifact it documents an intended configuration only; it does not by itself establish that the key was applied to the live registry, nor that the host communicated with that address - corroboration from the implant binary and registry/network artifacts would be needed.. This is the **corrected** finding — an initial, stronger reading was refuted by the Council (see *Self-correction*, below) and the analyst re-filed only what the evidence supports.

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01#NTFS inode 92256; $Recycle.Bin/S-1-5-...-1673/$R6SODDB/winclient.reg` — tool `sleuthkit icat + strings -el`, command `icat /tmp/nfury/win7-64-nfury-c-drive/win7-64-nfury-c-drive.E01 92256 | strings -el`
- **Cited evidence:** `[HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Netman\domain]`, `"home"="http://199.73.28.114/ads/"`, `"pause"=dword:00000020` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 d90cf223c7585a1c…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-009`.
- **Council Receipt:** `e5ae96abc830fb36…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

## What the evidence does NOT support (Council-enforced discipline)

The Council refused the following stronger readings because the available artifacts do not establish them.
They are recorded here explicitly so the report distinguishes **confirmed** from **inferred or unfounded**:

- Inference overreach (CERTAINTY): unjustified certainty — a single artifact rarely proves a conclusion outright. Offending clause: "reg file proves the intended configuration".

## Self-correction record (no human intervention)

- **F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-006** was drafted as: *"A recovered registry-import file (winclient.reg, NTFS inode 92256, recycled) co… → This .reg file configures malware command-and-control by writing under the Netman service…"*, then **bounced** by the Council:
  > [seat:inference] Inference overreach (CERTAINTY): unjustified certainty — a single artifact rarely proves a conclusion outright. Offending clause: "reg file proves the intended configuration".

  The analyst read the objection and self-corrected — re-filing the supported claim now shown under *Findings*.

## Confidence & limitations

- Confidence is stated per finding (HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / SPECULATIVE) and reflects what a single artifact
  can bear; nothing here is asserted beyond the cited evidence.
- "Observation" is the literal tool output; "interpretation" is the analyst's inference. The Council's
  Citation, Tool-semantics, Contradiction, and Inference seats independently checked the leap between them.
- Every finding traces to its tool execution (`csift trace F-analyst-SRL2015-NFURY-001`) and can be
  re-executed against the evidence (`csift trace --rerun …`); the Council Receipts are hash-chained.
- This narrative is generated from the audit substrate, not hand-written — it cannot contain a claim that
  is not a verified node in the graph.
